steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a quotation that illustrates Aristophanes' claim that sophists' practices and views are foolish. To illustrate means to show through a direct example — the quotation must come from a sophist's own words and make the foolishness plain on its face, not merely describe them or report secondhand that they are foolish.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Strepsiades says he will share a secret lesson': Strepsiades is a student of sophists, not a sophist himself. His boastful secrecy about the lesson is more comic than it is a direct illustration of sophists' foolish views. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Socrates asks if a customer has a good memory': Socrates is a sophist, but this line is a neutral intake question with no foolish reasoning on display. The foolishness of his views is not illustrated. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Strepsiades urges his son to become a sophist': Again, Strepsiades is the student, not a sophist. His financial motivation for pushing his son toward sophistry does not show a sophist's own foolish thinking. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Socrates explains why he suspends himself in a basket to study astronomy': Socrates is a sophist, and his explanation — that elevating himself physically allows his intellect to "mix with its kindred air" — is self-evidently absurd reasoning. A sophist's own words expose his foolish thinking directly. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
